subsystem: A PID-file writer, writepid(), for a Unix long-running daemon. It formats the running process's PID as an ASCII decimal string and (over)writes it into a caller-named pidfile on disk so init scripts and administrators can locate and signal the daemon. The pidfile path is the security-relevant object: it is opened, created if absent, truncated, written, and closed. The sample header comment 'primevul_c_validation_0401_fixed' and the file name '..._cwe59.c' mark this as the hardened (fixed) variant of a symlink/link-following (CWE-59) test case.
provenance: Identified by structure and idioms rather than an SPDX tag (none present). The bare function name writepid, the log_err(fmt, ...) logging shim, the O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC open with an optional O_NOFOLLOW guarded by #ifdef, and the EAGAIN/EINTR write-retry loop match the pidfile-writing helper found in NLnet Labs daemon code (Unbound/NSD family). The corpus path names the CWE under test: corpus/train/train_0083_cwe59.c, i.e. CWE-59 Improper Link Resolution ('Link Following'). This is presented as the 'fixed' sample, so the O_NOFOLLOW addition is the intended remediation.
trust_boundary: The process running writepid() is typically a daemon started as root (or a privileged service account) that must drop a pidfile into a shared, often world-writable or group-writable directory such as /var/run, /run, /tmp, or a package-controlled state directory. The attacker does NOT control the arguments pidfile or pid directly, but a local unprivileged attacker who can write to the containing directory (or any parent directory component of the path) controls the filesystem object the name resolves to: they can pre-plant a symbolic link, a hardlink, or a FIFO at the pidfile path before the daemon starts. The hostile inputs are therefore the on-disk state of pidfile and every intermediate directory in its path, not the byte contents written. The PID string itself (pidbuf) is trusted, bounded, daemon-generated data.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Line 1 is a corpus provenance comment (untrusted data, not an instruction). Line 2 is the function signature: writepid takes a const char* pidfile (the path to the pid file) and a pid_t pid (the process id to record). Note there is no explicit return type token shown before the name; the real source almost certainly declares 'static void writepid(...)' and the sample truncated the qualifier, since the body uses bare 'return;' with no value. The function's job is to persist 'pid' into the file named by 'pidfile'. Neither argument is attacker-supplied at the call site, but pidfile is a path that will be resolved against the live filesystem, which an attacker may have staged.

Local state: fd is the file descriptor; pidbuf is a fixed 32-byte stack buffer that will hold the formatted PID text; count tracks how many bytes have been written so far in the partial-write loop below. Line 7 formats the PID with snprintf, casting pid to 'unsigned long' and appending a newline. Using snprintf with sizeof(pidbuf) as the bound makes this write safe against overflow and always NUL-terminates: even a 64-bit unsigned long is at most 20 decimal digits, plus '\n' plus NUL = 22 bytes, comfortably inside 32. So the string-formatting step is not a memory-safety concern; the buffer is deliberately over-sized and bounded.

The core action: open the pidfile for writing, creating it if absent (O_CREAT) and truncating it to zero length if it already exists (O_TRUNC), with permission bits 0644 applied to a newly created file (subject to umask). The security-relevant part is the #ifdef O_NOFOLLOW block: when the platform defines O_NOFOLLOW, it is OR-ed into the flags so that open() refuses (with ELOOP) to traverse a symbolic link in the FINAL path component. This is precisely the CWE-59 hardening the sample name advertises. If open fails, the code logs the error via log_err with strerror(errno) and returns without touching fd further, so there is no descriptor leak on the failure path. Note there is no O_EXCL: the design intentionally tolerates a pre-existing pidfile (from a prior run) and truncates it.

A robust partial-write loop that keeps calling write() until the whole PID string has been emitted. pidbuf holds a constant string, so strlen(pidbuf) is invariant across iterations; count only ever advances by the bytes actually written (count += r, where r <= strlen(pidbuf)-count), so count can never exceed strlen(pidbuf) and the length argument strlen(pidbuf)-count never underflows. On write() error the code distinguishes transient failures (EAGAIN, EINTR) — which it retries via continue — from real errors, which it logs and breaks out of. This is correct handling of short writes and interrupted syscalls; there is no buffer arithmetic bug here because both operands are derived from the same bounded, trusted string.

The descriptor is closed on every path that reached a successful open (both the normal completion and the break-on-error path fall through to here), so fd is not leaked. The function returns void. There is no fsync/fdatasync, so the pidfile contents are not durably flushed before return, and — importantly — the function has no way to report success or failure to its caller: all error paths merely log and swallow the condition.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The symlink defense is compile-time conditional. On any platform (or build configuration) where the O_NOFOLLOW macro is not visible at this point — an old/embedded libc, a stripped uapi header, a target that never defined it, or an include-ordering accident — the #ifdef silently drops the flag and open() reverts to following symlinks in the final path component. Because this is a compile-time #ifdef with no runtime fallback (no fstat-after-open O_NOFOLLOW==0 check, no lstat/openat with O_NOFOLLOW enforced another way), the hardening can evaporate with zero warning. A local attacker who can create the pidfile path (its directory is commonly world- or group-writable, e.g. /var/run, /run, /tmp) pre-plants a symlink pointing at a victim file such as /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, a cron file, or another daemon's config. When the privileged daemon starts, open(O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC) follows the link and truncates the victim to zero length, then writes the PID digits into it — an arbitrary-file clobber and potential privilege escalation (CWE-59). Invariant the implementation must uphold: symlink refusal must be unconditional and enforced at runtime, not gated on a macro that may be absent.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if((fd = open(pidfile, O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC
#ifdef O_NOFOLLOW
		| O_NOFOLLOW
#endif
		, 0644)) == -1) {
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Even when O_NOFOLLOW IS compiled in, it only refuses a symlink at the LAST component of the path. Every intermediate directory in pidfile is still resolved normally, so if any parent directory is attacker-controlled (or is itself a symlink), the attacker redirects the whole resolution: e.g. if the daemon writes /var/run/mydaemon/mydaemon.pid and /var/run/mydaemon is a symlink the attacker planted to /etc, the final open still lands in /etc. O_NOFOLLOW does not protect against a symlinked directory component. Compounding this, O_CREAT is used WITHOUT O_EXCL and WITH O_TRUNC, so an existing target is unconditionally truncated; there is no fstat check that the opened object is a regular file the daemon owns, and no O_DIRECTORY/openat-with-resolve-beneath style hardening. The safe contract is to resolve and create the pidfile only under a directory the daemon controls with trusted permissions, ideally via openat(dirfd, ...) with RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS/O_NOFOLLOW and an fstat sanity check, and to treat a pre-existing non-owned target as an error (CWE-59, CWE-73 external control of path).

The create mode 0644 makes a freshly created pidfile world-readable. For a PID that is low sensitivity, but the more subtle issue is that a permissive mode combined with the missing O_EXCL means the daemon will happily adopt (and truncate/rewrite) a file another user pre-created; the mode argument is ignored entirely when the file already exists, so the attacker's chosen mode/ownership on a pre-planted file is what actually governs. The implementation should verify, after opening, that the object is a regular file owned by the daemon with expected permissions (fstat), rather than trusting the mode passed to open (CWE-732 incorrect permission assignment).

The loop treats only r == -1 as an error. A return of 0 from write() (no bytes written, not an error) leaves count unchanged and the loop condition count < strlen(pidbuf) still true, so the code spins calling write() forever with no progress and no exit — a potential 100%-CPU hang. For a regular blocking file this is essentially impossible (write of a non-zero count to a regular fd does not return 0), so the practical risk is low, but if fd ever refers to something exotic (a pipe/socket the path resolved to, e.g. an attacker-planted FIFO that O_NOFOLLOW does not block) a zero-return could stall the daemon (CWE-835 loop with unreachable exit condition). The safe contract is to treat r == 0 as a terminating/error condition. Separately, EAGAIN on a blocking descriptor should not occur, and looping tightly on it would also busy-spin, but the fd here is opened blocking so this is minor.

The function returns void and every failure path (open failure at line 16, write failure at line 25) only logs and returns/breaks; the caller receives no success/failure signal. A daemon that believes it wrote its pidfile but did not (permission denied, ELOOP from O_NOFOLLOW, disk full) will proceed as if healthy, breaking later stop/reload logic that reads the (missing or empty) pidfile. There is also no fsync before close, so a crash right after return can leave a zero-length pidfile even though O_TRUNC already destroyed the previous content — a small availability/consistency gap. Not a memory-safety bug, but a correctness/robustness contract the implementation should tighten by propagating an error status (CWE-252 unchecked/undelivered return status).

summary: This is the hardened variant of a pidfile writer whose only material security surface is symlink/link-following (CWE-59). The string formatting (snprintf into a 32-byte buffer) and the partial-write loop are memory-safe and correct. The residual risks are all filesystem-trust issues: O_NOFOLLOW is gated behind #ifdef so it silently disappears on platforms lacking the macro, and even when present it only guards the final path component while O_CREAT|O_TRUNC without O_EXCL and without an fstat sanity check lets a local attacker who controls the pidfile's directory (or a parent component) redirect the privileged daemon into truncating and overwriting an arbitrary file.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: writepid(const char* pidfile, pid_t pid) — function signature, line 2
reviewed_anchors.reason: void-returning; swallows all errors and cannot signal pidfile-write failure to the caller

reviewed_anchors.anchor: char pidbuf[32] — fixed stack buffer, line 5
reviewed_anchors.reason: Only ever filled by snprintf bounded to sizeof(pidbuf); max PID text (~22 bytes) fits with room to spare, always NUL-terminated

reviewed_anchors.anchor: size_t count — write progress counter, line 6
reviewed_anchors.reason: Advances only by bytes actually written and bounded by strlen(pidbuf); cannot underflow or overshoot
Bounded, self-terminating formatting of trusted daemon data; no overflow
O_NOFOLLOW guards only the final component and only when the macro is defined; O_CREAT|O_TRUNC without O_EXCL enables symlink/parent-dir redirection and arbitrary-file truncation
Compile-time gate means the symlink defense is silently absent on any platform/build where O_NOFOLLOW is not visible; no runtime fallback
World-readable and, more importantly, ignored for a pre-existing attacker-created target; no fstat ownership/type check

reviewed_anchors.anchor: write retry loop while(count < strlen(pidbuf)) — lines 18-28
reviewed_anchors.reason: r == 0 is not treated as terminating, giving a theoretical no-progress infinite loop
Both operands derive from the same bounded trusted string; length cannot underflow because count never exceeds strlen(pidbuf)
Correct transient-error retry; fd is blocking so EAGAIN is not expected, and EINTR retry is standard
Format string is a literal and args (pidfile, strerror(errno)) are valid; no format-string or NULL-deref hazard shown
Reached on all post-open paths; no descriptor leak; return value unchecked but write already completed

findings.title: Symlink following defense is compile-time optional (O_NOFOLLOW behind #ifdef)
findings.anchor: #ifdef O_NOFOLLOW | O_NOFOLLOW #endif around the open() flags
findings.code:
if((fd = open(pidfile, O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC
#ifdef O_NOFOLLOW
		| O_NOFOLLOW
#endif
		, 0644)) == -1) {
findings.bug_class: Improper link resolution / symlink following (arbitrary file truncation & overwrite)
findings.invariant: Refusal to open a symbolic link at the pidfile path must be UNCONDITIONAL and enforced at runtime — never dependent on whether a preprocessor macro happens to be defined for the target platform.
findings.failure_mode: On a platform/build where O_NOFOLLOW is not defined at this include point, the flag is dropped and open() follows a symlink in the final path component. A local attacker who can write the pidfile's directory pre-plants a symlink to a victim file; when the privileged daemon starts, O_CREAT|O_TRUNC follows it and truncates then overwrites that file with the PID digits — arbitrary-file destruction / privilege escalation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm O_NOFOLLOW is defined on every build target; if not, verify there is a runtime fallback (e.g. lstat/openat-with-fstat) so the defense cannot silently vanish. Check that build headers reliably expose O_NOFOLLOW where this compiles.

findings.title: O_NOFOLLOW guards only the final component; O_CREAT|O_TRUNC without O_EXCL allows redirection/truncation
findings.code:
if((fd = open(pidfile, O_WRONLY | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC
#ifdef O_NOFOLLOW
		| O_NOFOLLOW
#endif
		, 0644)) == -1) {
findings.bug_class: Link/path resolution via untrusted directory component; unconditional truncation of pre-existing target
findings.invariant: The pidfile must be created and opened only under a directory whose every component is trusted (daemon-owned, non-writable by others), and a pre-existing target must be validated (fstat: regular file, expected owner) before it is truncated — not blindly O_TRUNC'd.
findings.failure_mode: Even with O_NOFOLLOW, a symlinked or attacker-controlled PARENT directory redirects resolution to an arbitrary location; O_CREAT without O_EXCL plus O_TRUNC then truncates whatever real file the path resolves to. Attacker plants /var/run/<svc> as a symlink to /etc (or makes any parent writable), and the daemon truncates a victim file it did not intend to touch.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the pidfile's parent directory is created/owned by the daemon with mode denying other-user writes, or migrate to openat(dirfd, ..., O_NOFOLLOW) with RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS and an fstat check that the opened fd is a regular file owned by the daemon before writing.

findings.title: Create mode 0644 is world-readable and ignored for pre-existing attacker files; no fstat validation
findings.bug_class: Incorrect permission assignment / trusting mode on a possibly pre-created object
findings.invariant: The daemon must not rely on the mode passed to open() to guarantee the pidfile's permissions or type; after opening it should fstat the descriptor and confirm regular-file, expected owner, and expected mode.
findings.failure_mode: When the pidfile already exists (planted by an attacker), the 0644 mode is ignored entirely and the attacker's chosen ownership/permissions govern; the daemon writes into an object it never validated. Even in the benign case the pidfile is created world-readable.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether an fstat-based ownership/type check follows the open in the full implementation, and whether world-readability of the pidfile is acceptable for this service.

findings.title: write() returning 0 is not treated as terminating (no-progress loop)
findings.anchor: while(count < strlen(pidbuf)) { ssize_t r = write(...); ... count += r; }
findings.bug_class: Loop with potentially unreachable exit condition (busy-spin / hang)
findings.invariant: A write() return of 0 (no progress, not -1) must be handled as a terminating condition rather than retried indefinitely.
findings.failure_mode: If fd ever refers to an object where write() can return 0 with a nonzero count (e.g. an attacker-planted FIFO/pipe that path resolution reached), count never advances, the condition count < strlen(pidbuf) stays true, and the daemon spins forever at 100% CPU. Impractical for a plain regular file but a real availability edge case.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the loop breaks (or errors) on r == 0, and that fd cannot be a pipe/FIFO/socket via path resolution.

findings.title: Failure is silently swallowed; no status returned to the caller
findings.anchor: writepid(const char* pidfile, pid_t pid) — void return, log-and-return error paths
findings.invariant: A daemon that depends on a valid pidfile for shutdown/reload must learn whether the write actually succeeded; failure to open/write should propagate to the caller.
findings.failure_mode: open failure (line 16 return) or write failure (line 25 break) only logs; the caller proceeds believing the pidfile exists. Later stop/reload logic reads a missing or zero-length pidfile — after O_TRUNC already destroyed any previous content — degrading service management. No fsync means a crash after return can also leave an empty pidfile.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether the real signature returns an int/status and whether callers act on it; consider fsync before close for durability.

audit_checklist: Confirm O_NOFOLLOW is actually defined for every build target; add a runtime fallback (lstat/openat + fstat) so the symlink defense is never compiled out.

Ensure the pidfile's parent directory (and all path components) are daemon-owned and not writable by other users; prefer openat(dirfd, ..., O_NOFOLLOW)/RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS anchored to a trusted dirfd.
After open, fstat the descriptor and reject anything that is not a regular file owned by the daemon before truncating/writing (defends the no-O_EXCL + O_TRUNC path).
Reconsider O_CREAT without O_EXCL: for a fresh start, refuse to adopt an unexpected pre-existing pidfile rather than truncating it.
Treat write() == 0 as a terminating condition to remove the theoretical no-progress loop.
Propagate open/write failure to the caller (return status) and consider fsync before close for pidfile durability.
Confirm the create mode (0644) matches the service's confidentiality needs and is not being relied upon on a pre-existing file.
